evidence_chunks.claim: Use standardized anatomic classification, course, anterior chamber cell grade and activity terminology before comparing etiologies or treatment options.

HSV anterior uveitis becomes a stronger option in unilateral anterior uveitis with positive aqueous PCR for HSV, sectoral iris atrophy in a patient 50 years or younger, or HSV keratitis. These are classification criteria and do not replace clinical diagnosis.

VZV anterior uveitis becomes a stronger option in unilateral anterior uveitis with positive aqueous PCR for VZV, sectoral iris atrophy in a patient 60 years or older, or concurrent/recent dermatomal herpes zoster.

Fuchs uveitis syndrome should be retained as an option when the phenotype is chronic, usually unilateral, with characteristic iris changes and low-grade inflammation; the model must retrieve the full classification criteria before presenting this option as strongly supported.
HLA-B27/spondyloarthritis-associated anterior uveitis is supported by an acute or recurrent acute unilateral or alternating phenotype together with spondyloarthritis or a positive HLA-B27 test.

International expert consensus considers unilaterality, increased IOP, reduced corneal sensation and diffuse or sectoral iris atrophy useful clues for HSV/VZV anterior uveitis. Sectoral iris atrophy is particularly characteristic of HSV in that consensus.

For suspected HSV/VZV anterior uveitis, present an antiviral-based option plus inflammation and IOP control. Valacyclovir was preferred by most experts because of simpler dosing; topical corticosteroids and beta-blockers may be used when clinically indicated. Resolution of inflammation and normalization of IOP are treatment endpoints. Duration and recurrence management varied among experts.
safety_requirements: confirm_or_preserve viral etiology in differential
review renal function and dose adjustment before systemic antiviral
check pregnancy, allergy and interactions
do not present corticosteroid escalation without antiviral coverage when herpetic disease is suspected
exclude posterior herpetic involvement

evidence_chunks.claim: In suspected CMV anterior uveitis, diagnostic aqueous sampling should be offered as an option; in TITAN, 73.3% of participating experts reported always performing an aqueous tap in suspected cases.

For CMV anterior uveitis, present a topical antiviral option based on ganciclovir gel 0.15%. TITAN experts commonly paired antiviral coverage with prednisolone acetate 1% four times daily for one to two weeks, then adjusted treatment to clinical response.
safety_requirements: confirm availability and local regulatory status
assess corneal status and IOP
do not treat a suspected CMV phenotype as noninfectious uveitis alone
monitor response and reconsider diagnosis if inflammation or IOP does not improve

evidence_chunks.claim: Offer systemic valganciclovir as a separate physician-selectable option for severe, prolonged or atypical CMV anterior uveitis, not as an automatic default. Expert practice varied substantially.
safety_requirements: confirm diagnosis or very strong suspicion
review renal function
require blood-count and toxicity monitoring plan
check pregnancy and reproductive toxicity constraints
review interactions and local prescribing information

evidence_chunks.claim: Long-term maintenance, potentially up to 12 months, may be presented for chronic CMV inflammation or at least two episodes in a year, but the option must be labelled as consensus-based and individualized because practice varies.

For a phenotype judged noninfectious after appropriate exclusion of infection, present local anti-inflammatory therapy as a first-line option, with intensity and taper tied to activity and response rather than a fixed universal schedule. Monitoring must include IOP, lens status, anterior chamber activity and visual function.
safety_requirements: exclude or cover infectious etiologies when indicated
record baseline IOP
define early reassessment
avoid automatic taper without documented response

evidence_chunks.claim: For recurrent, chronic, steroid-dependent or sight-threatening noninfectious uveitis, present referral and systemic corticosteroid-sparing immunomodulatory therapy as an escalation option. The choice of agent requires disease-specific efficacy, safety screening, monitoring and shared decision-making.
safety_requirements: exclude infection and masquerade
coordinate with an appropriate specialist
complete pretreatment laboratory and infection screening
define toxicity monitoring and response criteria

evidence_chunks.claim: Do not allow routine treatment-option presentation to delay urgent evaluation when there is hypopyon after surgery or injection, suspected endophthalmitis, retinitis/vasculitis, severe visual loss, marked uncontrolled IOP with systemic symptoms, or significant posterior involvement.
